title: Gynecologic Oncology: A Systematic Approach
objectives: Understand the pathophysiology of Gynecologic Oncology
Identify key diagnostic features of Gynecologic Oncology
Apply clinical reasoning to Gynecologic Oncology management
Recognize common pitfalls in Gynecologic Oncology

sections.title: Overview of Gynecologic Oncology
sections.content:
**Gynecologic Oncology**

Cervical cancer screening and HPV, endometrial cancer risk factors, ovarian cancer tumor markers

**Key Points:**
• Postmenopausal bleeding = endometrial cancer until proven otherwise → transvaginal ultrasound + endometrial biopsy
• Endometrial cancer risk: unopposed estrogen, obesity, PCOS, tamoxifen, Lynch syndrome
• Cervical cancer: HPV 16/18 most oncogenic; Pap + HPV co-testing every 5 years age 30-65

sections.title: Pathophysiology & Key Features
sections.content:
• Postmenopausal bleeding = endometrial cancer until proven otherwise → transvaginal ultrasound + endometrial biopsy
• Endometrial cancer risk: unopposed estrogen, obesity, PCOS, tamoxifen, Lynch syndrome

sections.title: Diagnosis & Management
sections.content:
• Cervical cancer: HPV 16/18 most oncogenic; Pap + HPV co-testing every 5 years age 30-65
• Ovarian cancer: CA-125 tumor marker (not screening), BRCA1/2 association
• Cervical cancer staging is CLINICAL (not surgical); Endometrial and ovarian cancers are surgically staged

sections.title: Clinical Pearls
sections.content:
**High-Yield Points for Gynecologic Oncology:**

• Cervical cancer: HPV 16/18 most oncogenic; Pap + HPV co-testing every 5 years age 30-65
• Ovarian cancer: CA-125 tumor marker (not screening), BRCA1/2 association
• Cervical cancer staging is CLINICAL (not surgical); Endometrial and ovarian cancers are surgically staged

mcqs.question: Which of the following is MOST characteristic of Gynecologic Oncology?

options.text: Postmenopausal bleeding = endometrial cancer until proven otherwise → transvaginal ultrasound + endometrial biopsy
options.explanation: Postmenopausal bleeding = endometrial cancer until proven otherwise → transvaginal ultrasound + endometrial biopsy

options.text: Requires invasive testing in all cases

mcqs.question: What is the KEY diagnostic or management consideration for Gynecologic Oncology?

options.text: Cervical cancer staging is CLINICAL (not surgical); Endometrial and ovarian cancers are surgically staged
options.explanation: Cervical cancer staging is CLINICAL (not surgical); Endometrial and ovarian cancers are surgically staged

options.text: Immediate surgical intervention in all cases

mcqs.question: A patient presents with findings consistent with Gynecologic Oncology. Which finding would you expect?

options.text: Endometrial cancer risk: unopposed estrogen, obesity, PCOS, tamoxifen, Lynch syndrome
options.explanation: Endometrial cancer risk: unopposed estrogen, obesity, PCOS, tamoxifen, Lynch syndrome

mcqs.stem: A G2P1 at 38 weeks presents with painless bright red vaginal bleeding. Ultrasound shows a complete placenta previa. What is the recommended management?

options.text: Scheduled cesarean delivery at 36-37 weeks if stable; emergent C-section if hemorrhaging
options.explanation: Per ACOG, complete placenta previa is an absolute contraindication to vaginal delivery due to risk of life-threatening hemorrhage. If asymptomatic, planned cesarean at 36-37+6 weeks is recommended. Active hemorrhage at any gestational age requires emergent cesarean. At 38 weeks with bleeding, proceed to delivery.

options.text: Attempt vaginal delivery with close monitoring
options.explanation: Vaginal delivery is absolutely contraindicated with complete placenta previa — the placenta completely covers the cervical os, and cervical dilation would cause catastrophic hemorrhage.

options.text: Expectant management with bed rest until 40 weeks
options.explanation: At 38 weeks with active bleeding and complete previa, expectant management risks maternal hemorrhage. Delivery is indicated.

options.text: Digital cervical exam to assess dilation
options.explanation: Digital cervical exam is CONTRAINDICATED in placenta previa — it can disrupt the placenta and precipitate massive hemorrhage. Always confirm placental location by ultrasound before any cervical examination in third-trimester bleeding.
